items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: For a process to be spontaneous, what must happen to the total entropy of the universe?
items.correct: It must increase (ΔS_total > 0) — the system's entropy alone can decrease as long as the surroundings' entropy increases more.
items.misconception: The entropy of the system must increase for the process to be spontaneous.
items.random_wrong: The entropy of the surroundings must stay constant for the process to be spontaneous.
items.why: Students apply the second law only to the system, missing that what counts is system + surroundings (the whole universe) — which is why exothermic reactions can be spontaneous even with ΔS_system < 0.

items.q: When water boils, what is broken?
items.correct: Intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) between water molecules are broken — the O-H covalent bonds stay intact.
items.misconception: The covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen atoms are broken when water boils.
items.random_wrong: Water molecules split into hydrogen and oxygen gas when they boil.
items.why: Students don't distinguish between bonds within a molecule and forces between molecules, so they assume boiling 'breaks the molecule apart'.

items.q: Which state symbol means dissolved in water?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: (aq). “(s)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: are far apart and move fast
items.why: It's are far apart and move fast. “are fixed in place” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Water freezes at what temperature in °C?
items.why: The real figure is 0 °C. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
